coredata.dc:title: Arginine-modified carbon dots probe for live cell imaging and sensing by increasing cellular uptake efficiency
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Chen
Qian, Kun
Fu, Ailing
coredata.dc:description: As a new family of nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have been reported to enable to penetrate plasma membrane and show fluorescence in cells due to their small sizes and fluorescent properties. However, development of CDs as effective cellular imaging probes still remains a challenge, since they have relatively low efficiency of cell permeability which is difficult to be detected by a commonly used fluorescence microscope. Here we introduce arginine-modified carbon dots (Arg-CDs) with strong luminescence that could be used for cell imaging under common fluorescence microscope because of the high cellular uptake efficiency. More interestingly, the cellular luminescence showed red-shifting when the Arg-CDs entered cells, and the red luminescence emission ability depended on the cell lines, including NIH 3T3, HEK 293, Hela and MCF-7 cells. This finding opens up a new application field that the Arg-CDs could be used for cell imaging and sensing simultaneously as a fluorescent probe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Arg-CDs
Fluorescent probe
Cell imaging
Sensing
Fluorescence intensity

Time-course of cellular fluorescence after the Arg-CDs treatment
Fluorescence assay of cell media after addition of Arg-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work is supported by the grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81273416), the Scientific Research Foundation for Returned Scholars, Ministry of Education of China (2012-940), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2013A030) and the New Century Excellent Talents in University, Ministry of Education of China (2013-47).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characteristics of Arg-CDs. (A) Arg-CDs aqueous solutions under ultraviolet (330–380nm), blue (460–495nm) and green (530–550nm) light excitation of fluorescent microscopy; (B) Fluorescence spectra of Arg-CDs aqueous solutions under the excitation wavelengths from 360 to 700nm; (C) TEM image of Arg-CDs. scale bar is 20nm; the inset shows size distribution histogram.
FT-IR spectra of Arg-CDs, CDs and Arg. (A) Arg-CDs; (B) CDs; (C) Arg.

Fluorescence images of bare CDs and Arg-CDs in NIH 3T3, HEK 293, Hela and MCF-7 cells. The cells are observed under a fluorescence microscope following treated with bare CDs (A) or Arg-CDs (B) for 2h. Scale bar 20μm; (C) The total and separate fluorescence intensities in different cell lines as recorded with a microplate reader. The data were expressed as mean±SEM (n =6).
Time-course of cellular fluorescence after Arg-CDs treatment. (A) blue, (B) green and (C) red signal. Data were expressed as mean±SEM (n =3).
The fluorescence properties of Arg-CDs in cell media. Data were expressed as mean±SEM (n =6).
The viability of NIH 3T3, HEK 293, Hela and MCF-7 cells after co-incubated with Arg-CDs at 0.25mg/mL for 2h. Data were expressed as mean±SEM (n =3).

head.ce:title: Arginine-modified carbon dots probe for live cell imaging and sensing by increasing cellular uptake efficiency

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Education Ministry Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Education Ministry Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Plant Protection, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new family of nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have been reported to enable to penetrate plasma membrane and show fluorescence in cells due to their small sizes and fluorescent properties. However, development of CDs as effective cellular imaging probes still remains a challenge, since they have relatively low efficiency of cell permeability which is difficult to be detected by a commonly used fluorescence microscope. Here we introduce arginine-modified carbon dots (Arg-CDs) with strong luminescence that could be used for cell imaging under common fluorescence microscope because of the high cellular uptake efficiency. More interestingly, the cellular luminescence showed red-shifting when the Arg-CDs entered cells, and the red luminescence emission ability depended on the cell lines, including NIH 3T3, HEK 293, Hela and MCF-7 cells. This finding opens up a new application field that the Arg-CDs could be used for cell imaging and sensing simultaneously as a fluorescent probe.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite fluorescent quantum dots (QDs) have been widely used in the field of biomedicine research, such as drug delivery, bio-imaging and sensing [1–3], the concern for biosafety and health risk for the metal-based toxicities has led to the search for alternative nanoparticles [4]. As a new family of nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn considerable attention since their discovery in 2004 [5], due to their favorable biocompatibility and low toxicity. Currently, CDs have been successfully used as safe labeling probes for detecting DNA, metal ions, proteins in vitro based on their impressive properties such as stable photo-luminescence, broad excitation and tunable emission wavelength [6–8].

However, there are several drawbacks in the development of fluorescent CDs as optical imaging probes in live cells [9,10]. For example, synthetic CDs with long emission wavelength always cannot reach reasonable quantum yields (QYs) [11]; the blue or green luminescence possessed by most CDs with short emission wavelength have to overcome the problems of background interferences from organisms in bioimaging [12]. Additionally, the relatively low cellular fluorescence is another drawback for application of CDs in live cell imaging, though it is reported that CDs enable to penetrate plasma membrane and show fluorescence in cells. In order to extend the application of CDs in imaging, a great deal of attempts have been made and the studies mainly focused on the surface functionalization of CDs [13,14]. Positive ligands, including PEI, PEG and organosilane [15–17], have been reported to conjugate with naked CDs for increasing QYs.

Considering most of the synthetic procedures were somewhat lengthy and complicated, we were therefore seeking a simplified strategy to develop CDs as fluorescent probes for cell imaging that could be easily prepared in mild condition and for which the required reagents are readily available. We turned our attention to the basic amino acid—arginine (Arg), the derivatives of which are commonly considered to be able to increase cell uptake, because the positively charged guanidyl group in Arg could electrostatically bind with the negative components plasma membrane, and then promote the subsequent cell endocytosis [18]. We assumed that an arginine modified CDs (Arg-CDs) might have the capability of increasing the cell uptake and then could be used for live cell imaging.

The reaction condition to synthesize the bare CDs was fairly mild compared to the harsh reaction conditions for many other CDs, such as arc discharge, calcining in high temperature, laser ablation [19]. The facile hydrothermal method was employed to prepare bare CDs. Firstly, citric acid (1.057g) and ethylenediamine (335μL) were dissolved in distilled deionized water (10mL), then the solution was heated at reflux for 12h. After the reaction, the resulting solution was cooled down to room temperature. The greenish-black product was further subjected to dialysis (500–1000kDa dialysis tubing; Spectrum Labs), and the CDs with quantum yield of 55% was obtained as a greenish-black powder. Quinine sulfate (0.1M H2SO4 as solvent; QY=0.54) were chosen as standards. The QY was determined by slope method by the reference of quinine sulfate, comparing the integrated photoluminescence intensity and the absorbance value of the samples with that of the references [20]. Afterwards, the bare CDs were modified with Arg by using NHS and EDC [21]. Briefly, Arg (10mg) was dissolved into the MES buffer (20mL, 0.1M, pH5.6) with magnetic stirring, and then 23mg NHS (10mM) and 1.91g EDC (50mM) were added into the mixture to activate the carboxylic group. After 20min, the freezing-dried CDs powder (30mg) was added, and the mixture was stirred overnight at room temperature, subsequently, the resulting solution was purified by dialysis and Arg-CDs were obtained as a brownish solid.

The Arg-CDs were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM; Tecnai G2 S-TWIN, FEI, USA) and dynamic light scattering (DLS; Malvern). A microplate reader (Spectra Max M5, Molecular Devices, USA) was used for fluorescence intensity measurement. The functional information of Arg-CDs was made with a Nicolet AVATAR 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer.

Four different cell lines (NIH 3T3, HEK 293, Hela, MCF-7) were obtained from Peking Union Medical College (Beijing, China). The cells were respectively cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM, HyClone) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 100U/mL−1 penicillin and 100mg/mL−1 streptomycin at 37°C in 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere.

The inverted fluorescence microscopy (Olympus, Japan) was used to qualitatively determine the efficiency of cellular uptake. CDs or Arg-CDs (0.25mg/mL−1) from the stock solution were prepared with Dulbecco's Phosphate Buffered Saline (DPBS), and the solution (50μL) was added to the cell media, then incubated at 37°C in a 5% CO2 incubator for 2h. Before observation, the excess CDs and Arg-CDs were removed by washing 3 times with pre-warmed DPBS. Finally, the cells were observed under the fluorescence microscope and the images were taken with a digital camera.

The cells were transferred onto 24-well plates at a density of 5×104 cellswell−1 and incubated at 37°C, in 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere. When the confluence of cells reached 80%, CDs and Arg-CDs were respectively added into the growth media at the final concentration of 0.12mg/mL−1. Subsequently, the growth media was collected for fluorescence detection on a Molecular Devices Spectra Max M5 microplate reader. The cells were washed with pre-warmed DPBS, and then the cellular fluorescence intensities were measured with the M5 microplate reader.

The cells were respectively seeded in a 96-well plate, at a density of 2×105 cellswell−1 and incubated for 24h in the incubator (37°C, 5% CO2), and then the culture media were replaced with 100μL DMEM containing Arg-CDs. After incubation for 2h, the media containing Arg-CDs was removed, and replaced with 200mL fresh DMEM. The cell viability was measured according to the manufacturer's protocol of AlamarBlue cell viability assay reagent (Pierce). The fluorescence intensity was recorded on the microplate reader (Spectra Max M5). The wells with no CDs were used as the positive control, while the wells with no cells were considered as the blank control. The relative cell viability (mean±SEM, n =3) was expressed as [Abs (Sample−blank)/Abs (control−blank)]×100%.

The prepared Arg-CDs could freely disperse in water without extra ultrasonic dispersion, affording a transparent solution. Then, a tiny drop of the solution was placed on a cover glass, bright blue luminescence (Fig. 1A) was observed under the fluorescent microscopy at the ultraviolet excitation (330–380nm). To further investigate the luminescence feature of Arg-CDs, the emission spectrum was recorded as the strong blue luminescence peaking around 470nm by altering the excitation wavelengths (Fig. 1B).

The structure of Arg-CDs was uniform, monodispersed spherical particles through TEM image and the size distribution varied from 1.0 to 4.5nm (average diameter about 2.5nm) by TEM analysis (Fig. 1C). Furthermore, the functional groups on the surface of Arg-CDs were characterized by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2A, the broad bands at 3275cm−1 (νNH) and 1556cm−1 (δNH) were attributed to the vibration of NH2 groups. The stretching vibration of NCO (amide group) shifted to lower wavelength, compared with Arg and bare CDs (Fig. 2B and C), and the peak at 1651cm−1 was assigned to the amide linkage, indicating that Arg has been successfully conjugated to the surface of bare CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell uptake and color-shifting of Arg-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We here used four different types of cells, HEK293 (kidney; isogenic normal cell), NIH3T3 (fibroblast; isogenic normal cell), HeLa (cervical; cancer cell) and MCF-7 (breast; cancer cell) to identify the difference of the CD sensing between normal and cancer cells. Firstly, to investigate the cell uptake of CDs, the cells were incubated with cell media containing CDs for 2h, and then washed 3 times with DPBS and fresh cell media were added before observation. The results showed that all cells treated with bare CDs appeared almost undetectable fluorescence (Fig. 3A).

Since the cellular fluorescence intensity of bare CDs was so weak that cannot be used for cell imaging, Arg-modified CDs was subsequently attempted to increase the cell uptake. Contrary to bare CDs, the cells treated with Arg-CDs exhibited much stronger red fluorescence in cells (Fig. 3B), which can be easily detected by a commonly used fluorescence microscope. Interestingly, the strongest cellular fluorescence of Arg-CDs shifted to red as the blue fluorescence became very weak.

Moreover, the four cell types used in this study showed different fluorescence characteristics observed under fluorescence microscope or detected by microplate reader (Fig. 3B and C). For examples, NIH 3T3, HEK 293 and MCF-7 cells exhibited obvious red fluorescence; NIH 3T3 showed relatively strong green signals than other cell lines. Additionally, the three fluorescence intensities (red, green, blue) were significantly different among cell lines by statistics analysis of multiple comparison tests following one-way ANOVA (Fig. 3C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time-course of cellular fluorescence after the Arg-CDs treatment
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the characteristics of cellular fluorescence after the Arg-CDs treatment, the time-course of fluorescence intensities were determined. The results showed that the cellular fluorescence intensities increased following the incubation time, and reached the plateau in 1h (Fig. 4 ). The results showed that distinct fluorescence feature appeared in the detected cell lines after incubation with the Arg-CDs: NIH 3T3 cells showed significant red and green signals but the blue one was weak, while HEK 293 cells exhibited obvious blue and green signals except the red one was relatively weak; the overall intensity of Hela cells was the weakest and there was almost no blue fluorescence, and MCF-7 cells had distinct characteristics due to only strong red fluorescence could be detected in them. The results were consistent with the cell uptake of the Arg-CDs within different cells, suggesting that the Arg-CDs could be a cell sensor to distinguish cell lines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of cell media after addition of Arg-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To exclude the influence of cell media on red-shifting of the Arg-CDs, the cell media were collected and fluorescence intensities were measured by using a microplate reader. The results showed that Arg-CDs kept their native fluorescence property in the cell media as the strongest blue fluorescence (Fig. 5 ), and there was no significant different of fluorescence intensity between cell lines and control, suggesting that the fluorescence was shifted to red after Arg-CDs arrived in cells, which excluded the interaction of Arg-CDs and the contents of cell media.

Biocompatibility of CDs is one of the most important properties to be considered for their application in bioimaging and biolabeling in living cells. Hence, the cytotoxicities of Arg-CDs on the four cells were examined respectively. Actually, we have tested the fluorescence intensity and cytotoxicity after the addition of Arg-CDs into cell media from 30min to 24h, and it turned out that the fluorescence intensity was stable and the cells were in good condition (cell viabilities >92%). Here we selected 2h as a representative time point to show the cytotoxicity and fluorescence, and the results showed that there were slight cytotoxicity of Arg-CDs in cell lines, and the cell viability were 95.1%, 92.4%, 97.6% and 95.9% respectively (Fig. 6 ).

Here we developed a new Arg-CDs for cell imaging and sensing. Compared to the reported CDs, the signals of Arg-CDs in living cells could be detected by a commonly used fluorescence microscope because of high cell uptake efficiency, which should be easily popularized in biomedical applications. The actual uptake mechanism still remains unclear, but it's noted that the positive charge from a guanidyl group [C(NH)(NH2)2 +] in Arg could interact with the negative charge in cell membranes to promote endocytosis [22]. For example, the covalent conjugation of gold nanoparticles with Arg ligand for drug delivery has been considered as an efficient approach to increase cell uptake of the conjugations [23]. In this study, CDs functionalized with Arg significantly enhanced cellular uptake efficiency, which produced clear fluorescence and enough intensity for cell imaging.

The results of this study also suggest that the Arg-CDs could be used for sensing cell lines since the fluorescence exhibited red-shifting after they arrived in the cells, which is assumed that Arg-CDs might interact with the ingredients of cells [24,25]. One of the possible explanations might be associated with the increase of particle size to result in red-shifting [26,27]. In previous studies, the luminescence properties of CDs varied sensitively with their size: small CDs (1.2nm) gave UV light emission, while medium sized CDs (1.5–3nm) provided visible light emission (400–700nm), and large CDs (3.8nm) led to near-infrared emission [28]. The Arg-CDs in this study showed sphere and hemispheroid with the size 1.0–4.5nm (the average size ~2.5nm). When they arrived into cells, the negative proteins in the cytoplasm might be electrostatically bound with Arg positive group of Arg-CDs, leading to the larger size and then red-shifting. Also, it cannot exclude the energy transfer between Arg-CDs and cellular proteins [29], because it's also reported that the luminescence of CDs showed red-shifting when they were incubated with BSA under physiological condition due to energy transfer from CDs to BSA [30].

Despite it's difficult to distinguish cell types under microscope with naked eyes, molecular phenotypes underlies cell-specific in different cell lines that reflects their specific biological roles. Following the cellular luminescence red-shifting of the Arg-CDs, the intensity obviously changed dependent on cell lines probably due to specific proteins of different cell lines [31,32]. The four types of cells used in the study had individual originate, shape and specific function proteins, such as NIH 3T3 from mouse embryonic fibroblasts cells, HEK 293 from normal human embryonic kidney cells, MCF-7 and Hela as cancer cells from breast and cervix respectively. The results showed that the cell sensing of Arg-CDs was dependent on cell type, which was irrelevant to normal or cancer cells. When the Arg-CDs entered the cells, fluorescence intensities changed during red-shifting and each cell type showed their own specific features, for example, NIH 3T3 cells exhibited strong red and green fluorescence, while MCF-7 cell only appeared clearly red one. These results indicated that Arg-CD sensor distinguished the cell types through the changes of cellular fluorescence intensity. This Arg-CD sensor will provide a simple and effective method to recognize different cell lines of various tissue origins, with the potential of cell sensing and imaging. The development of the Arg-CD sensor also will be benefit for diagnosis of cancer cells from normal cells in biomedical application.

In conclusion, we suggest an effective fluorescent probe for cell imaging and sensing that can be directly detected by a common fluorescence microscope. The visualization approach is based on the first reported Arg-CDs, and the cellular uptake efficiency is obviously higher than bare CDs. More interestingly, the cellular luminescence showed red-shifting when the Arg-CDs entered cells, and the red luminescence emission ability depends on the cell lines, including NIH 3T3, HEK 293, Hela and MCF-7 cells. The cell viability study reveals that the Arg-CDs show reasonable biocompatibility on cells. This finding indicates that the Arg-CDs could be used for cell imaging and sensing simultaneously as a fluorescent probe.

C. Fu performed the experiments and wrote the paper, K. Qian also performed partial experiments and analyzed the data, A.L. Fu designed the study and wrote the paper. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is supported by the grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81273416), the Scientific Research Foundation for Returned Scholars, Ministry of Education of China (2012-940), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2013A030) and the New Century Excellent Talents in University, Ministry of Education of China (2013-47).
